● UNIVERSITY COLLEGE OF ENGINEERING (A) — OSMANIA UNIVERSITY B.E. BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
Syllabus reference & study planner

Engineering Mathematics,
semester by semester.

A complete, exam-ready breakdown of the four Mathematics courses on the B.E. (Biomedical Engineering) syllabus at Osmania University — unit-by-unit content, textbooks, and a week-by-week plan to actually get through it.

Courses coveredMath I – IV
Total credits12
DepartmentBME · UCE (A), OU
FormatSyllabus + Planner

How the maths ladder is built

Curriculum map
Note on curriculum vintage — Osmania University revises the BME scheme every few years. Under the current scheme (effective AY 2022–24), the BME programme carries exactly two dedicated mathematics courses: Engineering Mathematics‑I (Semester II) and Engineering Mathematics‑II (Semester III). Earlier CBCS-era schemes (2016–2020) carried a third course, Mathematics‑III, in Semester IV, and the older pre‑2015 scheme carried a fourth, Mathematics‑IV, in Semester V (III‑I). All four are reproduced here in full so you have the complete "Math I–IV" ladder however your batch's syllabus book numbers it — each card below is flagged with the academic year it belongs to.
MATH I

Sequences, Fourier & Multivariable Calculus

Semester II · BS201MT · Convergence tests, Fourier series, curvature, functions of several variables, double/triple integrals, vector calculus.

MATH II

Matrices, ODEs & Complex Analysis

Semester III · BS301MT · Eigenvalues & quadratic forms, first/higher-order ODEs, Bessel & Legendre functions, analytic functions, residues.

MATH III

Complex Analysis & PDEs

Semester IV (2016–20 scheme) · BS406MT · Analyticity, residue calculus, Fourier series, formation and solution of PDEs, heat/wave/Laplace equations.

MATH IV

Transforms & Statistics

Semester V (pre‑2015 scheme) · BM251UE · Complex variable theory, Z‑transforms, probability distributions, curve fitting, regression, hypothesis tests.

Engineering Mathematics – I

Semester II · AY 2022–23 scheme
BS 201 MT

Engineering Mathematics – I

Contact hrs/wk3 (L)
Credits3
CIE / SEE40 / 60
Course outcomes — find the nature of sequences & series · expand functions as a Fourier series · use multiple integrals to find area & volume of a bounded region · apply the toolkit (curvature, Jacobians, vector calculus) to curriculum-level problems.
01
Sequences & Series
Sequences, series, general properties of series, series of positive terms, comparison tests, D'Alembert's ratio test, Cauchy's nth root test, Raabe's test, logarithmic test, alternating series, absolute & conditional convergence. Fourier series, half‑range sine and cosine series, Parseval's theorem.
02
Calculus of One Variable
Rolle's, Lagrange's and Cauchy's mean value theorems (no proof), Taylor's series, curvature, radius of curvature, circle of curvature, envelope of a family of curves, evolutes & involutes, evaluation of definite and improper integrals, Beta, Gamma and Error functions.
03
Multivariable Calculus — Differentiation
Functions of two variables, limits & continuity, partial derivatives, total differential & differentiability, derivatives of composite/implicit functions (chain rule), change of variables, Jacobians, higher-order partial derivatives, Taylor's series in two variables, maxima/minima, Lagrange's method of multipliers.
04
Multivariable Calculus — Integration
Double integrals, change of order of integration, triple integrals, change of variables in integrals and applications to areas and volumes.
05
Vector Calculus
Scalar & vector fields, gradient, directional derivative, divergence and curl, line/surface/volume integrals, Green's theorem in a plane, Gauss's divergence theorem, Stokes' theorem (without proofs) and their verification.
Suggested reading R.K. Jain & S.R.K. Iyengar — Advanced Engineering Mathematics, Narosa, 4th ed. · Erwin Kreyszig — Advanced Engineering Mathematics, Wiley, 9th ed. · B.S. Grewal — Higher Engineering Mathematics, Khanna, 43rd ed. · G.B. Thomas, Weir & Hass — Thomas' Calculus, 12th ed. · B.V. Ramana — Higher Engineering Mathematics · N.P. Bali & M. Goyal — A Text Book of Engineering Mathematics · H.K. Dass & Rajnish Varma — Higher Engineering Mathematics, S. Chand.

Engineering Mathematics – II

Semester III · AY 2023–24 scheme
BS 301 MT · Common to all branches

Engineering Mathematics – II

Contact hrs/wk3 (L)
Credits3
CIE / SEE40 / 60
Course outcomes — solve systems of linear equations and eigenvalue problems · solve first-order and higher-order differential equations · determine analyticity of complex functions and expand them as Taylor/Laurent series · evaluate complex and real integrals using the residue theorem.
01
Matrices
Elementary row/column operations, rank of a matrix, echelon form, systems of linear equations, linear dependence/independence of vectors, linear & orthogonal transformations, eigenvalues, eigenvectors, properties of eigenvalues, Cayley–Hamilton theorem, quadratic forms, diagonalisation, reduction of a quadratic form to canonical form by orthogonal transformation, nature of quadratic forms.
02
First-Order Ordinary Differential Equations
Exact first-order differential equations, integrating factors, linear first-order equations, Bernoulli's, Riccati's and Clairaut's differential equations, orthogonal trajectories of a family of curves.
03
Differential Equations of Higher Orders
Linear independence/dependence, solutions of second and higher order linear homogeneous equations with constant coefficients, reduction of order for variable-coefficient equations, non‑homogeneous equations by variation of parameters, Euler–Cauchy equation, simultaneous linear differential equations, power series solutions, Legendre polynomials (first kind), Bessel's function (first kind) and their properties.
04
Functions of a Complex Variable
Limits & continuity, differentiability & analyticity, elementary analytic functions, necessary & sufficient conditions for analyticity, Cauchy–Riemann equations in polar form, harmonic functions, complex integration, Cauchy's integral theorem (incl. multiply connected regions), Cauchy's integral formula, Cauchy's inequality, Cauchy's formula for derivatives, Liouville's theorem, maximum modulus principle.
05
Residue Calculus
Power series, Taylor's series, Laurent's series, zeros & singularities, residues, residue theorem, evaluation of real integrals using residues, argument principle, Rouché's theorem, conformal mapping, bilinear transformations (all theorems without proof).
Suggested reading R.K. Jain & S.R.K. Iyengar — Advanced Engineering Mathematics, 4th ed. · Erwin Kreyszig — Advanced Engineering Mathematics, 9th ed. · B.S. Grewal — Higher Engineering Mathematics, 43rd ed. · M.D. Raisinghania — Ordinary & Partial Differential Equations, S. Chand · Brown & Churchill — Complex Variables and Applications, 9th ed. · B.V. Ramana — Higher Engineering Mathematics · S.L. Ross — Differential Equations, 3rd ed. · Simmons & Krantz — Differential Equations.

Mathematics – III

Semester IV · CBCS 2016–20 scheme
BS 406 MT

Mathematics – III

Contact hrs/wk4 (L+T)
Credits3
Sessional / Univ.30 / 70
Not on the current (2022–24) scheme — reproduced from the CBCS 2016–17 syllabus book for continuity of the Math I–IV ladder
Course outcomes — determine analyticity of complex functions and expand them as Taylor/Laurent series · evaluate complex & real integrals via residues · expand a function as a Fourier series · find solutions of first- and second-order partial differential equations.
01
Functions of Complex Variables
Limits & continuity, differentiability & analyticity, necessary & sufficient conditions for a function to be analytic, Cauchy–Riemann equations in polar form, harmonic functions, complex integration, Cauchy's integral theorem (incl. multiply connected regions), Cauchy's integral formula, Cauchy's formula for derivatives and applications.
02
Residue Calculus
Power series, Taylor's series, Laurent's series, zeros & singularities, residues, residue theorem, evaluation of real integrals using the residue theorem, bilinear transformation, conformal mapping.
03
Fourier Series
Fourier series, Fourier series expansions of even & odd functions, convergence of Fourier series, Fourier half-range series.
04
Partial Differential Equations
Formation of first- and second-order partial differential equations, solution of first-order equations, Lagrange's equation, nonlinear first-order equations, Charpit's method, higher-order linear equations with constant coefficients.
05
Fourier Series Applications to PDEs
Classification of linear second-order PDEs, separation of variables (Fourier method), Fourier series solution of one-dimensional heat and wave equations, Laplace's equation.
Suggested reading R.K. Jain & S.R.K. Iyengar — Advanced Engineering Mathematics, 4th ed. · B.S. Grewal — Higher Engineering Mathematics, 43rd ed. · Gupta & Kapoor — Fundamentals of Mathematical Statistics · Erwin Kreyszig — Advanced Engineering Mathematics, 9th ed. · James Brown & Ruel Churchill — Complex Variables and Applications, 9th ed.

Mathematics – IV

Semester V (III Yr – I Sem) · Pre-2015 scheme
BM 251 UE

Mathematics – IV

Contact hrs/wk4
Credits4
Sessional / Univ.25 / 75
From the 2009–10 syllabus book (III Year – I Semester) — the older four-course Math ladder, superseded by the current 2-course scheme
01
Functions of Complex Variables
Elementary functions of a complex variable, limit & continuity, analytic functions, Cauchy–Riemann equations, complex integration, Cauchy's theorem, derivatives of analytic functions, Cauchy's integral formula and its applications.
02
Taylor's & Laurent's Series Expansions
Zeroes & singularities, residues, residue theorem, evaluation of real integrals using the residue theorem, conformal mapping, bilinear transformation.
03
Z-Transforms
Introduction, basic theory of Z-transforms, Z-transforms of standard sequences, existence, linearity property, translation theorem, scaling property, initial & final value theorems, differentiation of Z-transform, convolution theorem, solving difference equations using Z-transforms.
04
Statistics
Random variables, distributions, density functions, conditional distributions, Bayes' theorem, mathematical expectation, expected values, moments & moment generating functions, Poisson / Normal / Gamma / Chi-Square distributions, curve fitting to data.
05
Curve Fitting by Least Squares
Correlation & regression, lines of regression, tests of significance — Chi‑Square, F and T tests.
Suggested reading R.V. Churchill — Complex Variables & its Applications, McGraw‑Hill · R.K. Jain & S.R.K. Iyengar — Advanced Engineering Mathematics · Miller & Freund — Probability & Statistics for Engineers, 3rd ed. · P.N. Arora, Sumeet Arora, S. Arora — Statistical Methods, 2nd ed. · Erwin Kreyszig — Advanced Engineering Mathematics, 8th ed. · H.K. Dass — Advanced Engineering Maths.

A working study plan

12 weeks per course · check items off as you go
Each course maps to 5 units across a ~13-week teaching term (12 weeks of instruction + revision). The plan below gives every unit roughly 2 weeks, with the final week reserved for previous question papers. Progress is saved locally in this browser, so you can close the tab and pick up where you left off.
0 / 0 weeks complete

General exam tactics

same across all four courses
01

Work from previous papers first

Osmania's Semester End Exam is a fixed pattern: a compulsory short-answer Part‑A across all units, plus long-answer Part‑B choices. Pull 3–4 years of question papers per course and map which units repeat.

02

Keep one theorem sheet per unit

For Math II–IV especially: write every named theorem (Cauchy, Rouché, Cayley–Hamilton, Parseval) with its statement only — no proof — on a single revision card. Most "state and explain" questions are graded on the statement.

03

Separate "solve" units from "prove" units

Matrices, ODEs, residues and Z-transforms are computation-heavy — drill problems. Analyticity, harmonic functions and PDE classification are proof/derivation-heavy — drill the logical steps, not just answers.

04

Reuse the toolkit across courses

Complex-variable theory in Math II reappears almost verbatim in Math III's residue unit. Fourier series from Math I reappears in Math III's PDE unit. Study them once, deeply, and cross-reference.

05

Do the "without proof" theorems last

Syllabi repeatedly flag certain results as examinable "without proof" (Stokes', Gauss divergence, Rouché's). Learn the statement and application cold; don't burn time reconstructing the proof.

06

Time-box CIE prep separately

Internal assessment (40 marks) usually draws from the first 2–3 units taught by mid-semester. Don't let SEE revision for later units crowd out CIE prep for earlier ones.

Sources

Primary syllabus documents
All unit content on this page is transcribed from the official scheme-of-instruction documents published by the Department of Biomedical Engineering, University College of Engineering (Autonomous), Osmania University:

· Math I — B.E. (BME) I & II-Semester Scheme of Instruction & Syllabus, AY 2022–23, uceou.edu/pdfs/biomedical.
· Math II — B.E. (BME) III & IV-Semester Scheme of Instruction & Syllabus, AY 2023–24, uceou.edu/pdfs/biomedical.
· Math III — B.E. (BME) IV-Semester Syllabus & Scheme of Instructions, CBCS AY 2016–17, uceou.edu/pdfs/biomedical.
· Math IV — B.E. III Year – I Semester Syllabus, AY 2009–10, uceou.edu/pdfs/biomedical.

Always cross-check against the current syllabus book issued to your batch — course codes, credit weightage, and unit order are periodically revised by the Board of Studies. The department's syllabus archive lives at uceou.edu/dept-bme.php.
University College of Engineering (A), Osmania University — Department of Biomedical Engineering
Built as a study reference · not an official university publication
